Off Calendar Apr-30-2018 Case Settled - Set For Crc 3.1385 Hrg On Jul-03-18 At 10:30 A.M. In Dept 610

Notice Of Motion And Motion To Bifurcate The Liability And Punitive Damages Phases Of Trial
Off Calendar Apr-06-2018 Denied
MINI MINUTES:
No appearances are made by or for parties in the case.

DEFENDANT TRADER JOE'S COMPANY Notice Of Motion And Motion To Bifurcate The Liability And Punitive Damages Phases Of Trial.

Trader Joe's Company's motion to bifurcate is DENIED WITHOUT PREJUDICE. Trader Joe's may renew the motion before the trial judge.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer, Jr.; Clerk: Sean Kane; Reporter: Reyes F. Hunter, CSR #6576, email: rhunter@sftc.org. (302/RBU)

MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT , Or In The Alternative, Summary Adjudication
Off Calendar Apr-04-2018 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Angela Rafoth, Esq. and Alexandra Hemenway, Esq. of Littler Mendelson PC, for defendant/moving party;
Keith A. Ehrman, Esq. of McGuinn Hillsman & Palefsky, T:415.421.9292, for plaintiff/opposing party, by Court Call.

RULING: The tentative ruling was contested and argued. At the conclusion of argument, the Court adopts the tentative ruling, except as to issue 2, as follows:

Defendant Trader Joe's Company's motion for summary judgment is denied and its alternative motion for summary adjudication in the alternative is denied as to the first and second causes of action (issue 1) and granted as to plaintiff Pedro Rosa's request for punitive damages (issue 3). There are triable disputes whether (1) lifting over ten pounds and frequent gripping, grasping, and pulling with his right hand were essential duties of the position of a crew member and (2) Mr. Rosa could have performed these assertedly essential duties with or without reasonable accommodations. The court need not address whether there is evidence that Trader Joe's failed to provide reasonable accommodations for Mr. Rosa's claimed disability and failed to engage in good faith in the interactive process since there is a triable dispute regarding the essential functions of the job Mr. Rosa held at the time of his termination. (Abara v. Altec Industries, Inc. (ED CA 2011) 838 F.Supp.2d 995, 1004-1005). However, were the court to address those issues, there are triable disputes as to them as well. The court also need not address whether Mr. Rosa failed to mitigate his economic damages since CCP 437c does not permit summary adjudication as to some but not all compensatory damages. Were the court to address that issue, there is a triable dispute whether Mr. Rosa exercised reasonable diligence in seeking paid employment after Trader Joe's terminated his employment. Trader Joe's is entitled to summary adjudication in its favor as to punitive damages because, even assuming that Ms. Baca was Trader Joe's managing agent, the undisputed facts show that Trader Joe's termination of Mr. Rosa's employment did not constitute malice, oppression or fraud within the meaning of those terms as defined in Civil Code 3294.

The Court will also issue an order on defendant's objections to evidence.

Counsel for Mr. Rosa is to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the Court's ruling and also complies with CCP 437c(g) and CRC 3.1312.

Judge: Harold Kahn; Clerk: G. Gonzales; Reporter: Reyes F. Hunter, CSR#6576, email: rhunter@sftc.org.
